name: Kullervo
description: Kullervo is a tragic hero from Finnish mythology, featured prominently in the Kalevala. Born into a world of betrayal and hardship, Kullervo's early life was marked by slavery and abuse at the hands of Ilmarinen, the renowned smith. He possessed immense strength and a volatile temper, which often led to unintended consequences and devastating outcomes. Kullervo's inability to control his rage and his constant quest for revenge against those who wronged him define his character. He is known for his accidental incestuous relationship with his sister, a pivotal event that drives him to despair. Ultimately, Kullervo's life is a testament to the destructive power of vengeance and the tragic consequences of a life devoid of love and guidance. His story serves as a cautionary tale, highlighting the dangers of unchecked emotions and the importance of empathy and understanding. Despite his flaws, Kullervo remains a compelling figure, embodying the darker aspects of the human condition and the enduring power of fate.
subtitle: Tragic hero of vengeance and fate.
achievements: Became a symbol of tragic fate.

keyMyths.title: Kullervo's Enslavement
keyMyths.description: Kullervo is sold into slavery to Ilmarinen, where he is mistreated by Ilmarinen's wife. This fuels his hatred and desire for revenge, setting the stage for his tragic life.

keyMyths.title: The Incestuous Encounter
keyMyths.description: Kullervo unknowingly seduces his own sister, a devastating event that leads to her suicide and further fuels his despair. This act is a turning point in his tragic downfall.

keyMyths.title: Kullervo's Suicide
keyMyths.description: Overwhelmed by grief and guilt, Kullervo returns to the place where he seduced his sister and takes his own life with his sword. This act marks the final tragic end to his story.

sources.author: Elias Lönnrot
sources.description: The primary source for Finnish mythology and the story of Kullervo.
sources.text: The primary source for Finnish mythology and the story of Kullervo.
